Lambertian surface interaction
Adjust the controls to change the incoming ray, surface normal, wavelength, and reflected intensity.

plan.contextChunk:
how light interacts with surfaces and how those interactions are
recorded with a camera.

## Light Interacting with Surfaces {#sec-light_interacting_with_surfaces}

Visible light is electromagnetic radiation, exhibiting wave effects like
diffraction. For many imaging models, however, it is helpful to
introduce the abstraction of a **light ray**, describing the light
radiation heading in a particular direction from a particular location
in space (@fig-lightSpray). A light ray is specified by its position, direction, and
intensity as a function of wavelength and polarization. In this
treatment, we ignore diffraction effects.

![A light ray from the sun strikes a surface and generates outgoing rays
of intensity and color depending on the angles of the incoming and
outgoing rays relative to the surface orientation.](figures/imaging/brdf.png){#fig-lightSpray width="75%"}

Let an incident light ray be from direction $\mathbf{p}$ and of power,
$\ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda)$, as a function of spectral wavelength $\lambda$ ().
The power of the outgoing light, reflected in the direction,
$\mathbf{q}$, is determined by what is called the **bidirectional
reflection distribution function** (BRDF), $F$, of the surface. If the
surface normal is $\mathbf{n}$, then outgoing power is some function,
$F$, of the surface normal, the incoming and outgoing ray directions,
the wavelength, and the incoming light power:
$$\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}}, \mathbf{n}, \lambda, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q} \right)
$$ {#eq-Lambert}

### Lambertian Surfaces

In general, BRDFs can be quite complicated (see @Matusik2002),
describing both diffuse and specular components of reflection. Even
surfaces with completely diffuse reflections can give complicated
reflectance distributions @Oren1994. A useful approximation describing
diffuse reflection is the **Lambertian model**, with a particularly
simple BRDF, which we denote as $F_L$. The outgoing ray intensity,
$\ell_{\texttt{out}}$, is a function only of the surface orientation relative to the
incoming and outgoing ray directions, the wavelength, a scalar surface
reflectance, and the incoming light power:
$$
\ell_{\texttt{out}} = F_{L} \left( \ell_{\texttt{in}} (\lambda), \mathbf{n}, \mathbf{p} \right)  = a \ell_{\texttt{in}}(\lambda) \left( \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{p} \right),
$${#eq-lambert}

where $a$ is the surface **reflectance**, or
**albedo**, $\mathbf{n}$ is the surface normal vector, and $\mathbf{p}$
points toward the source of the incident light. Note that the brightness
of the outgoing light ray depends on the orientation of the surface
relative to the incident ray, as well as the reflectance, $a$, of the
surface. For a Lambertian surface, the intensity of the reflected light
is a function of the direction of the incoming light ray, but not a
function of the outgoing direction of the ray, $\mathbf{q}$.

elements: flat green surface (rectangle)
surface normal vector (black arrow labeled n, originating at intersection point)
incident light ray (colored arrow, yellow-orange, labeled l_in(λ), coming from above at a slant)
outgoing light ray directions (multiple green arrows radiating from the intersection point, one bold and labeled q)
point of intersection (small black ring/circle at surface)
incoming direction vector p (bold black arrow labeled p, along incident ray)
labels for vectors p, q, n
mathematical annotation: l_out = F(l_in, n, λ, p, q)

interactions.label: Incident angle θ_in
interactions.teaches: Shows how changing the angle of incoming light affects the orientation of vector p and the intersection with the surface.

interactions.label: Surface normal tilt
interactions.teaches: Demonstrates how rotating the surface normal (vector n) alters the relationship between the incoming ray and the surface.

interactions.label: Wavelength λ (nm)
interactions.teaches: Alters the color of the incoming ray to reflect different visible wavelengths, connecting physics to ray color.

interactions.label: Reflectance a
interactions.teaches: Models how the reflectance (albedo) affects the intensity of the outgoing rays, visualized by their brightness.

interactions.label: Lambertian mode
interactions.teaches: Toggles between the general BRDF and the simpler Lambertian reflection, showing how outgoing light behavior changes.

demo_steps.title: Single Light Ray and Flat Surface
demo_steps.narration: Here, a single colored light ray strikes a flat, green surface at a moderate angle. The surface normal (n) is vertical and the incident direction (p) aligns with the ray. Outgoing rays scatter into multiple directions, illustrated by green arrows. This setup models the basic interaction in the BRDF framework.

demo_steps.title: Changing Surface Orientation
demo_steps.narration: Now, as we tilt the surface normal, watch how the angle between the incoming ray and the normal changes. This alters the dot product n⋅p, which is fundamental in determining the outgoing intensity—the brunt of the interaction in both general and Lambertian BRDFs.

demo_steps.title: Lambertian Diffuse Reflection
demo_steps.narration: Switching to Lambertian mode, notice that all outgoing directions are now shown equally bright, regardless of which way they leave the surface. The reflected intensity depends only on the angle between n and p and the surface reflectance, not the outgoing direction q. This demonstrates the defining property of Lambertian reflection.

demo_steps.title: Varying Wavelength and Reflectance
demo_steps.narration: Let’s now change the wavelength and reflectance. Watch the incident ray change color to reflect different parts of the spectrum, while increasing the reflectance makes all outgoing light brighter. This illustrates how surface color and reflectance together shape the color and intensity of light leaving the surface.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Start with an isometric view, slightly above and to one side of the surface so the incoming ray and outgoing vectors are clearly visible. Enough zoom to read labels and see arrows distinctly.
interactionPlan.notes: Outgoing rays' color/brightness should be visibly modulated by reflectance and n⋅p; smoothly animate vector rotations and arrow brightness. Color of incoming ray adjusts with wavelength slider using a visible spectrum colormap.

attempts.html:
Single Light Ray and Flat Surface
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The outgoing green arrows (representing outgoing directions) are not all shown with the same boldness or orientation as in the original, and one is thicker and more prominent, which is not directly emphasized in the source.
The wavelength gradient on the incident ray is less visually smooth and prominent compared to the striking yellow-red gradient in the source figure.
The font and layout of the HTML equation label does not perfectly match the style, sizing, or exact position of the source equation.
The ring at the intersection point is more prominent in the 3D version and was not as visually focal in the source.
UI controls and extra interactive labels are present that are not in the original static source image.
evaluation.notes: The core geometry and all key labels are accurate and pedagogical, with strong interactivity, but some minor mismatches in arrow boldness, color gradients, and label typography remain.
action_items: Make the incident ray color gradient sharper and more consistent with the vivid yellow-to-red in the source.
Adjust all outgoing direction arrows to match thickness, color opacity, and prominence as in the original (avoid over-emphasizing only one).
Use math-style serif fonts and match the layout and size more closely for equation and vector labels.
Reduce the thickness of the ring or intersection point indicator to better match the subtlety of the source.
If possible, offer a toggle to hide UI controls for a mode closely matching the static figure.

feedback.rationale: All action items focus on visual, labeling, and UI execution improvements rather than issues with the underlying plan or conceptual structure.
action_items: Make the incident ray color gradient sharper and more consistent with the vivid yellow-to-red in the source.
Adjust all outgoing direction arrows to match thickness, color opacity, and prominence as in the original (avoid over-emphasizing only one).
Use math-style serif fonts and match the layout and size more closely for equation and vector labels.
Reduce the thickness of the ring or intersection point indicator to better match the subtlety of the source.
If possible, offer a toggle to hide UI controls for a mode closely matching the static figure.
actionItems: Make the incident ray color gradient sharper and more consistent with the vivid yellow-to-red in the source.

If possible, offer a toggle to hide UI controls for a mode closely matching the static figure.

attempts.html:
Lambertian surface interaction
Adjust the controls to change the incoming ray, surface normal, wavelength, and reflected intensity.
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The label ℓ_out=F(ℓ_in, n, λ, p, q) is much larger and overlaps the plane, slightly mispositioned compared to the source.
The outgoing arrows have a heavy green color and partial transparency, whereas the source uses lighter green arrows with no transparency.
The background plane and ring are thicker and more pronounced than in the source.
The rainbow gradient of ℓ_in mimics the original but is slightly less smooth and visually more segmented.
Extra UI controls, buttons, and tooltips are present that do not appear in the reference image (though these are for interactivity).
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive fidelity with only minor mismatches in label placement, arrow coloring, and graphical plane styling.
action_items: Fine-tune the sizing and placement of the ℓ_out label to avoid overlapping the plane and better match the reference.
Adjust the green outgoing arrows to be less saturated and reduce transparency for closer visual alignment.
Make the green plane's outline thinner and lighten the black ring, making the 3D appearance subtler like the original.

feedback.rationale: All action items relate to visual styling and placement tweaks rather than conceptual errors or plan structure.

Make the green plane's outline thinner and lighten the black ring, making the 3D appearance subtler like the original.

discrepancies: Green plane surface is highly faithful in color and orientation.
Incident arrow has correct gradient from yellow to orange, matching the source.
All key arrows (n, p, q) and outgoing green arrows are present with correct positioning and color.
Mathematical labels ℓ_in(λ), ℓ_out=F(...) and n, p, q are present with correct font, size, and placement.
Slight excess of interface controls and extra Lambertian narration not present in original static figure.
evaluation.notes: Extremely faithful reproduction with accurate geometry, labels, and meaningful interactive controls that improve on the textbook figure.
action_items: Refine outgoing arrows so their angular density and opacity more closely match the original (slightly adjust opacity and number if desired).
Optionally reduce UI clutter for presentations focusing only on a static or explanatory version.
Keep advanced interactions but offer a 'static' mode toggle for simpler lecture/slideshow export.
